q: Energy demand is the term used to describe the consumption of energy by human activities.
Energy demand forecasting is crucial for ensuring future economic growth and environmental security.
The global demand for energy has decreased in recent years due to industrial development and population decline.
The supply of energy is currently far greater than the actual demand.
The energy crisis is the concern that the world's demands on the limited natural resources that are used to power industrial society are diminishing as the demand rises.
An energy crisis situation can threaten economic and national security due to increasing energy prices.
With ninety percent of the world's oil reserves already discovered, people need not find new ways to make energy.
Non-renewable resources of the energy will last long and can be easily regained, preventing a future energy crisis.
Renewable energy has a negligible potential to provide a solution to increase energy crisis.
Energy demand influences the total amount of energy used and the locations and types of fuel used in the energy supply system.
Energy demand depends on socioeconomic factors such as population, urbanization, and industrialization.
The characteristics of the technologies that consume energy are not influenced by energy demand.
Demand for energy is expected to decrease year by year because population and new technology are stable.
Renewable energy is a key factor to the future of energy, food and economic security.
A nation suffering from a disruption of energy supplies connected by decreasing energy prices faces an energy crisis.
Non-renewable resources are expected to last long enough that the world will not face an energy crisis in the near future.
The primary purpose of energy demand forecasting is to manage immediate energy consumption, not future security.
Human activities primarily describe the consumption of energy, referred to as energy demand.
Energy is considered a luxury rather than a basic necessity for life.
The energy crisis is solely a concern for developed industrial societies, not for the entire world's limited natural resources.
